title: Enhancing Benefits Enrollment
tagline: Replaced a manual workflow with a centralized, self-managed platform; cutting processing time by 72%.

admin_pain_points: Benefits changes required running multiple payroll cycles and manual reconciliation.
Admins had no easy way to aggregate employee data across departments in one place.
When enrollment spikes occurred, there was no clear escalation or alert workflow.
user_pain_points: Adding new employees required support tickets and manual development effort.
Data needed to be collected, validated and sent across teams. Changes were tracked through emails.
No centralized governance or clear permission structure.

constraints: Balancing the product's existing patterns with user needs without disrupting the experience.

steps.label: Empathize
steps.description: User interviews & stakeholder sessions

steps.label: Define
steps.description: Problem framing & pain point mapping

steps.label: Ideate
steps.description: Flows, architecture & wireframing

steps.label: Prototype
steps.description: Hi-fi screens & interactive prototypes

steps.label: Test
steps.description: Post-launch Hotjar analysis

design_strategy: Define the underlying architecture and logic before changing the UI.
Find what parts of the system work well and build around those.
With no design system, collaboration with the development team ensured the forward-facing UI remained cohesive throughout.

reflections.title: Architecture before UI.
reflections.body: Investing time in mapping the permission model before designing screens prevented costly rework. The logic had to be airtight before any pixel moved.

reflections.title: Trust through constraints.
reflections.body: Working without a design system forced tight collaboration with engineers. Constant syncs kept the UI cohesive and the handoff smooth even under timeline pressure.

nda_notice: Parts of this presentation,including some screens and project details,have been redacted or blurred due to a confidentiality agreement signed with the client. The work shown is real; full details are withheld to protect client privacy.

title: Nayya Integration
tagline: Designed the integration of Nayya as an AI-powered benefits tool, guiding employees to choose coverage that matched their needs.

metrics.label: IRR
metrics.context: Projected ROI,estimated return from increased plan uptake (NPV $11.4M).

metrics.label: Conversion Rate
metrics.context: Users who received Nayya recommendations that fit their needs and enrolled in a plan.

admin_pain_points: A seamless embedded integration between Flex and Nayya Choose was not feasible under the technical constraints.
The team needed to balance business value, technical limitations, and user confidence during a sensitive decision-making process.
user_pain_points: Employees needed more support when selecting benefits and choosing coverage that matched their needs.
Users would need to leave Flex and access Nayya Choose in a separate experience.
The transition needed clearer communication, stronger context, and visual cues that made the handoff feel intentional and trustworthy.

constraints: A seamless embedded integration between Flex and Nayya Choose was not technically feasible.
The project moved on an aggressive timeline with significant technical limitations.
The experience had to protect user confidence during benefits decision-making.

steps.label: Understand
steps.description: Clarified the benefits selection journey and Nayya Choose's role in decision support.

steps.label: Define
steps.description: Identified the feasible integration path between Flex and the external Nayya experience.

steps.label: Align
steps.description: Collaborated with the Solution Leads, Product Onwer, Business Analyst and Devs.

steps.label: Validate
steps.description: Recommended user testing to understand concerns before finalizing the handoff.

steps.label: Measure
steps.description: Used enrollment behavior and revenue projections to evaluate impact.

design_strategy: Define a realistic integration path instead of forcing an embedded experience that the platform could not support.
Use communication and visual cues to explain where users were going, why the transition mattered, and how Nayya Choose supported their benefits decisions.
Protect continuity between Flex and Nayya Choose by making the handoff feel intentional, trustworthy, and aligned with the broader enrollment journey.

reflections.title: Designing under constraints.
reflections.body: Designing under technical constraints does not mean compromising the experience. It means finding the most effective path within the boundaries you are given.

reflections.title: Test early, fix early.
reflections.body: User testing, even at a small scale, surfaces the decisions that matter most before they become expensive to fix.

reflections.title: Trust is a design problem.
reflections.body: When users cross platform boundaries, clarity and context are what keep them moving forward.

reflections.title: Constraints force focus.
reflections.body: The inability to embed Nayya natively pushed the team to invest in transition design, an area that is often overlooked when seamless integration feels like the obvious solution.

content_blocks.title: Team
content_blocks.body: I worked closely with the UX Lead and Content Designer to ensure consistency in both experience and messaging under a tight timeline. I also collaborated with the Product Owner, Business Analyst, and Solution Leads to align the proposed experience with business goals and technical realities.

content_blocks.title: Situation
content_blocks.body:
Employees needed more support when selecting benefits, and Nayya Choose offered a strong recommendation engine to guide them toward more relevant coverage.

The project moved on an aggressive timeline and came with significant technical limitations. A seamless embedded integration between Flex and Nayya Choose was not feasible.

content_blocks.title: Task
content_blocks.body:
I was responsible for helping define the best integration path between the two platforms while protecting the user experience as much as possible.

This meant balancing business value, technical constraints, and user confidence during a sensitive decision-making process.

content_blocks.title: Actions
content_blocks.body:
Because a seamless integration was not possible, we identified that users would need to leave Flex and access Nayya Choose in a separate experience.

To reduce friction, I helped recommend user testing to better understand concerns before finalizing the experience.

The research showed users needed clearer communication about where they were going, why the transition mattered, and visual cues that made the handoff feel intentional and trustworthy.

content_blocks.title: Impact
content_blocks.body: The integration showed clear value for users who engaged with Nayya Choose. Participants who completed the survey enrolled in twice as many benefits as those who skipped it, and the work contributed to measurable growth across medical, ancillary, and PEO revenue.

insights: • Participants who got recommendations from Nayya: 5.44
opportunities: Less than 7% of eligible employees completed a Nayya survey, leaving strong room for growth.
Increasing completion to 10% could add 10,000+ plans and generate $2M-$7M in incremental PEO benefits revenue.
Further research opportunities include deeper analysis of Nayya survey behavior and year-over-year enrollment changes.
Potential product expansion areas include 401(k), FinFit, and PayActiv recommendations.

title: Flock Accessibility Audit & Design System Unification
tagline: Led an accessibility audit and helped unify the design system. This reduced pattern debt, made the product more consistent, and improved collaboration between design and development teams.

metrics.label: Shared foundation
metrics.context: aligned patterns, typography, icons, and dialogs

admin_pain_points: The product had accessibility risk across core flows and no mature shared library to address issues systematically.
Teams were working with duplicated patterns, inconsistent naming, and fragmented component behavior across pages.
Weak documentation and handoff ambiguity created implementation friction and made system decisions harder to scale.
user_pain_points: Inconsistent components made the experience less predictable from page to page.
Accessibility gaps reduced clarity and usability for people relying on stronger hierarchy, focus treatment, and consistent interaction states.
Dialogs, icons, typography, and responsive behavior lacked enough shared rules to feel cohesive across the product.

constraints: Accessibility improvements had to happen while the product was live and already fragmented across multiple pages and patterns.
There was no fully mature design system in place, so audit work and system definition had to progress together.
The system needed to support real engineering constraints, QA, and implementation readiness rather than remain a Figma-only cleanup effort.

steps.label: Audit
steps.description: Checked for accessibility issues and components that didn't match the system.

steps.label: Structure
steps.description: Grouped problems by importance to see what to fix first.

steps.label: Systemize
steps.description: Created clear patterns, states, and rules for how components should work.

steps.label: Align
steps.description: Worked with developers to make sure designs could be built correctly in React.

steps.label: Validate
steps.description: Reviewed designs and builds, using feedback to improve consistency.

design_strategy: Clarify foundations, components, patterns, and modules so teams know when to use each pattern and how it should behave.
Separated accessibility fixes from design-system decisions by documenting patterns, variants, states, and usage rules.
Worked closely with developers to adapt component decisions to implementation realities, QA needs, and handoff clarity.
Used design review, accessibility checks, and implementation feedback to strengthen adoption and consistency.

reflections.title: Usability is about revealing what matters.
reflections.body: Usability isn't about removing; it's about revealing what matters. This project proves that accessibility, performance, and UX all strengthen each other when treated as one discipline.

content_blocks.title: Team
content_blocks.body: I led the audit framing, defined which patterns needed consolidation, and partnered with engineering on how components should behave in build. I worked alongside another designer, in collaboration with a UX Lead, UX Manager, Product Owner, Business Analyst, three developers, and a Dev Lead.

content_blocks.title: Situation
content_blocks.body:
The lack of a design system meant icons, typography, dialogs, repeated interface patterns, inconsistent component behavior, and reusable UI patterns had drifted over time.

That created friction for implementation, affected accessibility readiness, made handoffs less reliable, and increased the cost of maintaining consistency in the experience.

content_blocks.title: Task
content_blocks.body:
My role was to turn accessibility findings and UI inconsistencies into a clear system direction to define reusable patterns that design and engineering could adopt.

The product had been recently acquired, so the goal was to keep its visual identity while improving consistency and usability through a more scalable, React-based system.

Success meant reducing duplicated patterns, improving the product, clarifying handoff expectations, and creating stronger accessibility guidance for future work.

items: I built an audit framework that grouped issues by severity, system impact, and implementation complexity, while separating accessibility findings, system inconsistencies, and engineering constraints for clearer decision-making.

I reviewed foundations and shared patterns across icons, typography, dialogs, and components to identify where states, variants, naming, and responsive behavior needed to be standardized. From that, I defined reusable patterns and clearer guidance in Figma to reduce ambiguity and improve handoff consistency.
I also partnered closely with developers during review and QA to adapt patterns to real technical constraints and confirm that the proposed system decisions worked in build.

content_blocks.title: Product/System Outcomes
content_blocks.body: The project created a clearer system foundation for accessibility, component reuse, and implementation consistency. Because confirmed business metrics were not preserved, the outcomes are framed through product, system, and delivery signals.

insights: The audit made severity and prioritization visible, which helped the team address structural accessibility problems instead of isolated UI fixes.
Shared guidance for icons, typography, dialogs, and reusable patterns reduced inconsistency and gave the system a clearer source of truth.
Defining states, variants, and usage rules reduced ambiguity between design and engineering and improved the quality of implementation conversations.
projected: A clearer adoption path for reusable components, patterns, and modules.
More reliable handoff and QA because accessibility notes and usage guidance are documented.
Lower risk in future accessibility reviews by addressing issues systemically instead of page by page.
Pattern consistency helps users understand the interactions on the pages.
successMetrics: Reduction of duplicated or conflicting component patterns.
Improved components’ states, variants, and responsive behavior across reused modules.
Fewer implementation questions during handoff and QA.
Accessibility issues identified and resolved by severity before release.

title: I-9 & E-Verify Integration for Flex
tagline: Integrated I-9 and E-Verify into Flex onboarding, improving task completion by 12% and user confidence from 3.2 to 4.0.

metrics.label: of Flex clients lacked a compliant I-9 workflow

admin_pain_points: Clients often relied on manual processes or custom off-platform solutions to manage I-9 and E-Verify requirements.
Admins needed clearer status tracking, document access, vendor handoff visibility, and transaction-based billing tied to onboarding.
The experience had to support multiple user roles, future scalability, and compliance-driven workflows.
user_pain_points: New hires needed to complete Section 1 of Form I-9 as part of onboarding without feeling disconnected from the Flex experience.
Users needed stronger cues before leaving Flex for the external HIREtech experience.
Employees and admins needed clearer ownership, progress states, completion feedback, and follow-up actions.

constraints: The workflow depended on HIREtech, a third-party vendor experience outside Flex.
The design had to account for employee and admin journeys, compliance constraints, vendor redirects, billing requirements, and status tracking.
The product needed to scale for shared workers, same-FEIN clients, white-label support, and migration of existing I-9 documents.

steps.label: Research
steps.description: Conducted design explorations and testing across user journeys.

steps.label: Map
steps.description: Mapped where the workflow should begin in Flex and where users would transition into HIREtech.

steps.label: Align
steps.description: Aligned I-9 with familiar onboarding task patterns like W-4 and direct deposit.

steps.label: Clarify
steps.description: Defined handoff moments, status visibility, task ownership, and completion feedback.

steps.label: Scale
steps.description: Considered future needs including shared workers, same-FEIN clients, white-label support, and document migration.

design_strategy: Use clearer handoff messaging, progress states, and completion feedback to reduce confusion around the external HIREtech experience.

reflections.title: What worked.
reflections.body: Anchoring a compliance-heavy workflow to familiar onboarding patterns made I-9 feel native to the product rather than a bolted-on task. A single usability test with 7 users was enough to shift a product decision and protect a better experience,data beats opinion. Combining iterative testing with clear handoff strategy created a stronger foundation for both usability and business growth.

reflections.title: What I’d do differently.
reflections.body: Establish third-party alignment earlier,waiting on compliance validation from HIREtech created rework and slowed design iterations. Moving faster through compliance content decisions by setting clearer approval checkpoints upfront, rather than iterating in circles, would have saved time.

reflections.title: What this unlocked.
reflections.body: The project exposed a missing pattern in Flex for communicating third-party transitions to users. Solving it here created a reusable transparency pattern that addressed a systemic problem affecting multiple integrations across the platform.

content_blocks.title: Situation
content_blocks.body:
Flex clients were completing I-9 and E-Verify requirements outside the platform, relying on manual coordination, off-platform tools, and admin follow-up to close compliance gaps.

Around 76% of users were affected, creating inconsistent completion rates, higher error risk, and increased support burden across hiring workflows.

Without a native workflow, there was no reliable way to track status, confirm ownership, or ensure compliance was completed on time.

items: Owned the end-to-end UX process as sole designer, from research to final handoff.
Designed for both the employee journey (Section 1) and the admin journey (Section 2 and status tracking).
Integrated I-9 and E-Verify into Flex without fragmenting the existing onboarding experience.
Facilitated a working session with BAs, engineers, and product leads to surface open questions, integration constraints, and third-party gaps with HIREtech.

delivery_items: Delivered detailed design specs for development including behaviors, content examples, component definitions, and edge case documentation.
Designed email communications to announce the new integration and follow up with users who did not complete the I-9 process.
Created handoff guidelines documenting how the Flex to HIREtech integration was communicated to users, covering redirect messaging, progress states, and completion feedback.

insights: Task completion rate,69% (early concept) to 81% (refined concept).
Status interpretation errors down from 31% to 22%.
User confidence score improved from 3.2/5 to 4.0/5.
Manual process dependency baseline established at 60–70%; target set at under 25% for post-launch tracking.
2,548 survey responses post first release confirmed the integrated direction was working.
opportunities: Employees leaving mid-flow had no way to save progress, creating drop-off risk and incomplete I-9 submissions.
Adding form save state could directly improve completion rates and reduce admin follow-up for unfinished cases.
Content misalignment between HIREtech and Flex was identified in testing; updated communication guidelines were delivered and awaiting HIREtech confirmation.
The transparency pattern developed for this integration opens the door to applying the same handoff strategy across other third-party workflows in Flex.

title: Reverse Health Quiz Funnel Diagnosis
tagline: A 44-step quiz funnel selling a calisthenics program for women was converting at just 0.63%, and the team needed to determine whether the visible problem, the long middle, was actually the real bottleneck.

metrics.label: Total quiz steps
metrics.context: Full end-to-end funnel length from paid click to purchase.

metrics.label: End-to-end conversion
metrics.context: Overall purchase conversion across the full funnel.

metrics.label: Mid-funnel steps under suspicion
metrics.context: The long quiz middle that appeared to be the likely source of drop-off.

admin_pain_points: The funnel was converting at only 0.63% end to end, creating pressure to optimize quickly without clear evidence about where the real breakdown occurred.

Because the experience contained 44 steps, the team could easily default to the most visible explanation, that the quiz was simply too long, without first proving whether that was the dominant problem.
user_pain_points: Most visitors were arriving cold from Google Ads, so every step had to justify why they should keep going before enough trust had been built.
The middle of the funnel contained 37+ quiz steps, making it easy to assume the interaction cost itself was pushing users out.
Without a sharper diagnosis, any redesign risked polishing the middle while ignoring weaker parts of the funnel before or after it.

steps.label: Map
steps.description: Documented the full funnel from Google ad click through the 44-step quiz to purchase, identifying where the team believed friction was highest.

steps.label: Question
steps.description: Challenged the default assumption that the long middle was the primary issue, reframing the problem as a funnel-level diagnosis instead of a step-count complaint.

steps.label: Break down
steps.description: Separated the experience into acquisition promise, entry experience, quiz progression, and post-quiz transition so each part could be evaluated on its own terms.

steps.label: Prioritize
steps.description: Focused the team on identifying which section of the funnel was truly leaking intent before committing to a redesign direction.

steps.label: Frame next moves
steps.description: Defined the core problem statement the next design phase should solve instead of assuming the answer was to shorten the quiz.

design_strategy: Treat the 44-step flow as a system rather than a single UI problem, because low conversion can come from weak acquisition-to-landing alignment, not just excessive step count.
Resist optimizing the most painful-looking section until the funnel is broken into diagnosable stages with clearer hypotheses.
Use problem framing to protect the team from solving the wrong problem too early.

reflections.title: The obvious problem is not always the real one.
reflections.body: A long quiz is easy to blame because it is visible. That does not automatically make it the highest-leverage place to intervene.

reflections.title: Cold traffic changes the standard.
reflections.body: Visitors arriving from Google Ads have not earned much trust yet, so every part of the funnel has to work harder than it would in a warm audience context.

reflections.title: Diagnosis is design work.
reflections.body: Before changing layouts or reducing steps, the team needed a sharper understanding of where intent was actually breaking down.

nda_notice: Some funnel details are summarized for portfolio use, but the structure, traffic context, and conversion problem reflect the real engagement.

content_blocks.title: Team
content_blocks.body: I worked across growth and product conversations to help frame the real problem before design effort was spent in the wrong place. The work required alignment between acquisition thinking, funnel behavior, and user experience diagnosis.

content_blocks.title: Situation
content_blocks.body:
Reverse Health was running a 44-step quiz funnel that sold a calisthenics program for women. End to end, the funnel was converting at 0.63%.

Traffic was mostly cold paid search, with a typical visit beginning from a Google ad. Inside the team, the most intuitive explanation was that the long quiz middle, more than 37 steps, had to be where users were dropping.

content_blocks.title: Task
content_blocks.body:
The scope of the work centered on funnel diagnosis and design improvements, paywall experiment design, and competitor pattern extraction.

My role was to interpret funnel data, identify UX and conversion problems, and translate those insights into concrete, testable design solutions inside a performance-driven product environment.

content_blocks.title: Task 1
content_blocks.body: Rather than treat every step as equal, I read all 44 and picked 3 to examine, balancing churn, user volume, and downstream influence. Then for each I dug into two questions: why it performs the way it does, and what user behavior, friction, or clarity issue explains the data.

analyses.title: 31. Age Step - Good Performance
analyses.body: A clear, low-effort step. By this point abandoning the flow would feel wasteful, so users push through. The cognitive and emotional load is minimal, there's no financial decision attached, and most people understand that age matters in a health plan.
analyses.consideration: Age is asked twice, on the landing and again at step 31. Testing it on a single step could cut effort and shorten the funnel.

analyses.title: 4. Main goal - Regular Performance
analyses.body: The single-select option keeps it light and avoids decision overload for users. The friction is more subtle: this is the moment the user tells the product what outcome they want, so naming one goal carries emotional weight. A hypothesis, based on research, is that selecting a goal adds emotional weight to this step, and that weight can feel like pressure, especially when the options don't fully align with the user or they haven't decided yet.
analyses.consideration: Test a version with different content options, and add an "I haven't decided yet" choice. Also reduce the number of steps before this question, so users reach their goal-setting moment sooner, while intent is still high.

analyses.title: 34. Enter Email - Bad Performance
analyses.body: This is the second biggest in funnel leak, and it's the worst possible place to lose people. At this point users can't yet see the value of the product, so asking for an email without explaining the benefit can feel like a marketing capture step rather than part of the personalized plan experience.
analyses.consideration: Some users also sense the paywall coming. Handing over an email right before a likely payment ask makes the wall feel closer, so a chunk bail preemptively.

content_blocks.title: Impact
content_blocks.body: This phase sharpened the problem statement: a long middle may be part of the issue, but it should not be treated as the answer before the full funnel is diagnosed. The immediate value of the work was creating a better frame for what to test and redesign next.

insights: The quiz middle felt like the problem because it was long and highly visible.
Cold paid search traffic raised the cost of every commitment in the funnel, not just the quiz steps themselves.
The most important outcome of this phase was reframing the work from "shorten the quiz" to "diagnose the real bottleneck first."
opportunities: Compare drop-off and intent quality between the landing entry, the quiz middle, and the post-quiz purchase transition.
Test whether the ad promise and landing expectation are aligned well enough for cold search traffic before asking users to commit to a long flow.
Identify which moments in the quiz earn momentum and which ones merely add interaction cost.
